summary: Introduce a Tauri desktop shell for rust-diffforge with a placeholder login screen and a backend health ping to the hosted Next.js API.

review_items.summary: The previously empty Rust project becomes a packaged Tauri desktop app with Rust host code and a vanilla JavaScript WebView frontend.
Development runs through the native Tauri window while a loopback-only Vite server provides quiet hot reloading inside the WebView.
Production builds create a downloadable Windows NSIS installer instead of only building a browser website preview.
Add a Tauri command that performs a bounded GET request to https://diffforge.ai/api/hello and returns a compact connection status to the WebView.
rust-diffforge becomes a consumer of the existing public Next.js /api/hello endpoint through the hosted diffforge.ai API URL.
The desktop login UI is intentionally non-authenticating until a reviewed backend auth contract exists, so typed credentials are not sent to an invented route.
No database schema, migration, SQL policy, or data access change is required.

patches.summary: Add npm scripts and JavaScript frontend dependencies for native Tauri dev, quiet WebView hot reload, and installer packaging.
Add Rust package manifest for the Tauri host.
Add the Tauri runtime and backend_ping command.
Configure Tauri dev to use Vite HMR and production builds to emit a Windows NSIS installer.
Add a development override that lets Tauri attach to an already-running Vite server.
Add the placeholder login UI behavior and command invocation.

compatibility.notes: The Rust app only consumes an existing public API route and does not change the Next.js producer contract.

cross_project_impact.compatibility_notes: The desktop app treats the endpoint as a connectivity check only. Auth remains unimplemented until a dedicated backend route is reviewed.

tests.summary: Install desktop frontend and Tauri CLI dependencies.
